titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the John Willis Collection of Lakota Art
titlestmt.author: Matthew Daniel Mason

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This finding aid, which is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0, has a MARC record in Yale's ILS with the following bib number: 15896281.
This collection contains 4 published digital objects. And those objects are comprised of 6 digital files.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday December 5, 2024 at 23:03

did.unittitle: John Willis Collection of Lakota Art

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.physdesc: 8 boxes, 1 broadside
did.abstract: Drawings and inkjet prints of drawings created by Lakota artists, 2008-2017, as well as art produced by others related to protests against the construction of the Dakota Access Pipeline in North Dakota, 2016-2019. Artists represented in the collection include Gilbert Kills Pretty Enemy, III, Quinton Maldonado, Joe Pulliam, and Dwayne Wilcox. It includes 25 original drawings as well as 20 inkjet prints of drawings. More than half of the works created by the artists integrate ledger paper. Six works related to protests of the Dakota Access Pipeline chiefly consists of screen-prints on cloth, which includes an example designed by Dignidad Rebelde, a collaborative graphic arts project of Jesus Barraza and Melanie Cervantes.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The John Willis Collection of Lakota Art is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from John Willis on the Walter McClintock Memorial Fund, 2020.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into five series: I. Gilbert Kills Pretty Enemy, III, 2016. II. Quinton Maldonado, 2015-2016. III. Joe Pulliam, 2017-2019. IV. Dwayne Wilcox, 2008-2016. V. Dakota Access Pipeline Protest Art, 2016-2019.

bioghist.head: John Willis (born 1957)
bioghist.p: John Willis is a documentary photographer and emeritus professor of photography at Marlboro College in Marlboro, Vermont. His monographs include Recycled Realities , co-authored with Tom Young (Center for American Places, 2002), Views from the Reservation (Center for American Places, 2010), and Mni Wiconi - Water Is Life Honoring the Water Protectors at Standing Rock and Everywhere in the Ongoing Struggle for Indigenous Sovereignty (Staunton, Virginia: George F. Thompson Publishing, 2019). Willis has photographs in the permanent collections of the Whitney Museum of American Art, the Museum of Fine Arts in Boston, and the Museum of Fine Arts in Houston, among others. He is also a co-founder with Bill Ledger of the In-Sight Photography Project, a photography outreach project to youth in Brattleboro, Vermont.

bioghist.head: Gilbert Kills Pretty Enemy, III (born 1979)
bioghist.p: Gilbert Andrew Kills Pretty Enemy, Ill, is a Hunkpapa Lakota artist from the Standing Rock Reservation. His artwork includes drawing, graphic art, silk screening, tattooing, tribal art, wood burning and painting. He earned two associate degrees from United Tribes Technical College including art marketing in 2001 and small business management in 2005. He is the founder of Chameleon Horse Art & Design at McLaughlin, South Dakota.

bioghist.head: Quinton Maldonado (born 1982)
bioghist.p: Quinton Jack Whiting Maldonado is an Oglala and Sicangu artist. As a child, he lived on the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation with his maternal grandparents Margaret Chips Jack Maldonado (1914-1987) and Manuel Vasquez Maldonado (1922-1989). He has created art since childhood, and after viewing the work of Donald F. Montileaux (born 1948) while in high school, Maldonado has studied and created ledger drawings.

bioghist.head: Joe Pulliam (born 1968)
bioghist.p: Patrick Joel Pulliam, also known as Akicita Tokahe (First Soldier/ Warrior), is an Oglala Lakota artist born at Pine Ridge, South Dakota, and raised in the Oglala Lakota Nation at the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation. After service in the United States Army, in 2003 Pulliam began in-depth studies of his Lakota heritage and focused on ledger drawing after an introduction by artist Daniel Long Soldier (born 1949).

bioghist.head: Dwayne Wilcox (born 1957)
bioghist.p: Dwayne "Chuck" Wilcox is an Oglala Lakota artist born at Kadoka, South Dakota, and raised at Wanblee on the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation. He served in the United States military. A self-taught artist, Wilcox created his first commissioned artwork in 1974 and transitioned to a fulltime art career in 1987 with an emphasis on ledger drawings. Example of his work are collected in Visual/Language: The Ledger Drawings of Dwayne Wilcox (Staunton, Virginia: George F. Thompson Publishing, 2021), edited by Karen Miller Nearburg.

bioghist.head: Ledger Drawings
bioghist.p: Ledger drawings are traditionally representational drawings of scenes from the lives of nineteenth century Plains Indians, with an emphasis on martial accomplishments. Ledger drawings were so named for the ruled ledger books in which they were often executed which the creators obtained from soldiers or traders. Many twentieth century and twenty-first century Native American artists have also integrated ledgers in their work.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: John Willis Collection of Lakota Art. Yale Collection of Western Americana, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit. These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Beinecke Manuscript Unit Processing Manual.

scopecontent.head: Description of the Collection
scopecontent.p: Drawings and inkjet prints of drawings created by Lakota artists, 2008-2017, as well as art produced by others related to protests against the construction of the Dakota Access Pipeline in North Dakota, 2016-2019. Artists represented in the collection include Gilbert Kills Pretty Enemy, III, Quinton Maldonado, Joe Pulliam, and Dwayne Wilcox. It includes 25 original drawings as well as 20 inkjet prints of drawings. More than half of the works created by the artists integrate paper from ledgers. Six works related to protests of the Dakota Access Pipeline chiefly consists of screen-prints on cloth, which includes an example designed by Dignidad Rebelde, a collaborative graphic arts project of Jesus Barraza and Melanie Cervantes.
Collection is organized with drawings and inkjet prints under the names of Lakota artists.

relatedmaterial.head: Associated Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Dwayne Wilcox, Ledger Drawings (WA MSS S-4448). Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

geogname.part: Standing Rock Indian Reservation (N.D. and S.D.)
Standing Rock Sioux Tribe of North & South Dakota

did.physdesc: (2 boxes)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: Drawings and Inkjet Prints of Drawings.

title.part: Incredible Hulk Fights the Black Snake

did.physdesc: 1 drawing, ink and crayon on paper, 29 x 45 cm, in window mat, 44 x 61 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Drawing on ledger paper for "Chattel Mortgages Filed" created by an unidentified corporate body, 1891-1898; signed and dated on recto by the artist

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Incredible Hulk Fights the Black Snake , Box 2, folder 6

did.physdesc: 1 drawing, ink and, crayon on paper, 29 x 45 cm, in window mat, 44 x 61 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Drawing on ledger paper for "Chattel Mortgages Filed" created by an unidentified corporate body; signed and dated on recto by the artist

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Morton County Sheriff's Last Stand , Box 2, folder 7

1 drawing, ink on paper, 35 x 22 cm

scopecontent.p: Drawing on ledger paper for "George L. Mayer, District Manager" created by an unidentified corporate body, 1936-1939; titled, signed, and dated on verso by the artist; accompanied by board with description of the drawing by the artist

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Drawing on a promissory note of the National Park Bank, Jamestown, New York, August 1915; signed and dated on recto by the artist

scopecontent.p: Drawing on a double-spread of ledger paper for an index "Chattel Mortgages Filed" created by an unidentified corporate body, 1893; signed and dated on recto by the artist

did.physdesc: (2 boxes)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: Drawings and Inkjet Prints of Drawings.

title.part: 500 Years of Making America Great Again

did.physdesc: 1 drawing, ink, crayon, and colored pencil on paper, 29 x 45 cm, in window mat, 45 x 61 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Drawing on a blank sheet of paper from an ledger for an electoral board, approximately 1900; signed and dated on recto by the artist

title.part: After A Long Day of Clubbing, Dog Tired & Its Miller Time

did.physdesc: 1 drawing, ink, crayon, and colored pencil on paper, 26 x 43 cm, in window mat, 41 x 61 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Drawing on a blank sheet of paper from a ledger for judgments at a circuit court printed by Brown and Saenger, Sioux Falls, South Dakota, approximately 1900; signed and dated on recto by the artist

did.physdesc: 1 drawing, ink, crayon, and colored pencil on paper, 27 x 43 cm, in window mat, 42 x 58 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Drawing on a blank sheet of paper from a ledger for the treasurer of Missoula County, Montana, approximately 1950; variant of a work also known as Under the Microscope , 2014; signed and dated on recto by the artist

title.part: Fake News Report, They Say If You Take A Picture It Lasts Longer

did.physdesc: 1 drawing, ink, crayon, and colored pencil on paper, 26 x 43 cm, in window mat, 41 x 58 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Drawing on a blank sheet of paper from a ledger for judicial cases in Gregory County, South Dakota, approximately 1950; variant of a work known as Under the Microscope , 2014

did.physdesc: (1 box)

title.part: Letter from Harold C. Frazier to TransCanada, July 12, 2018

did.physdesc: 1 inkjet print, 28 x 23

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Reproduction of a letter on letterhead of the Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe by Harold C. Frazier, 1966-, which includes a single line in its body: "We will be waiting"; for reference, the folder includes a copy provided by library staff of a July 11, 2018, letter by TransCanada, to which Frazier replies

title.part: Protect Mother Earth, #NODAPL, Water is Life

did.physdesc: 1 screen print on fabric, 51 x 33 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a depiction of Sitting Bull, 1831-1890, also known as Tȟatȟáŋka Íyotake, from a photograph created in 1883 by D. F. (David Francis) Barry, 1854-1934

title.part: Protect the Water, Defend the Land

did.physdesc: 1 screen print on fabric, 51 x 34 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Artwork by Dignidad Rebelde, a collaborative graphic arts project of Jesus Barraza and Melanie Cervantes

title.part: Seal of the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe of North & South Dakota

did.physdesc: 2 stickers, acrylic, 19 x 19 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Stylized version of the official seal

title.part: Tó éí iiná, Water is Life

did.physdesc: 1 screen print on fabric, 48 x 32 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Tó éí iiná" is Diné for "Water is Life"

did.physdesc: 1 screen print on fabric, 24 x 32 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Mní Wičhóni" is Lakota for "Water is life"

title.part: We are Here to Protect, Water is Life

did.physdesc: 1 screen print on fabric, 32 x 28 cm
